review.summary: The patch fixes the immediate invalid-server dereference path in `icp_irq()`, but the new error path likely implements the wrong recovery semantics. The commit message says invalid IVE/server values should be ignored, yet the code calls `ics_reject()`, which re-injects/requeues an interrupt rather than dropping a malformed guest routing target. That can preserve a bad state and lead to repeated delivery attempts or log spam instead of safely discarding the malformed interrupt.

stages.A: The reported bug is credible: `icp_irq()` must not assume that a guest-provided server id resolves to a valid ICP target.
stages.B: The added `!icp` guard is in the right place to stop invalid state access before the normal priority/path logic runs.
stages.C: The recovery action is inconsistent with the stated fix. `ics_reject()` is a retry/backpressure path, not an 'ignore invalid value' path, so malformed routing may remain live.
stages.D: Static checks are clean and configure succeeds, but the patch does not demonstrate a malformed-IVE regression test or explain why requeueing invalid targets is safe.
stages.E: Revise the invalid-server branch to explicitly drop/quiesce the interrupt source, or document and test why `ics_reject()` is the correct semantic for permanently invalid routing data.

findings.title: Invalid-server path rejects instead of ignoring malformed routing
findings.description: The new guard correctly detects that `server` does not map to a valid ICP target, but the follow-up action uses `ics_reject(ics, nr)`. In XICS, reject is normally the path for temporary delivery failure and can cause the interrupt to be reconsidered later. For a malformed IVE/server field, that is not equivalent to 'ignoring invalid values' as described in the commit message, and it risks repeated retries or persistent guest-error logging against a permanently bad destination.
findings.suggestion: Use a drop/quiesce path for malformed routing instead of `ics_reject()`, or update the implementation and commit message together with a test proving that requeueing an invalid target cannot loop or repeatedly resurface the same bad interrupt.
findings.confidence_reason: The null-check itself is straightforward, but the semantic mismatch between 'ignore invalid values' and `ics_reject()` is visible directly in the patch and aligns with typical XICS reject semantics as a retry-oriented path.

diff:
From git@z Thu Jan  1 00:00:00 1970
Subject: [PATCH] As reported in qemu-project/qemu#3324
From: kiki <Chan9Yan9@gmail.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Mar 2026 23:25:57 +0800
Message-Id: <20260326152557.344808-1-chan9yan9@gmail.com>
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: text/plain; charset="utf-8"
Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit

A malformed IVE value can result in an invalid server field being
passed to icp_irq(). The function assumes the server id is valid and
may access invalid state otherwise, potentially leading to a crash.

Fix this by validating the server id before using it and ignoring
invalid values.

Reported-by: Zexiang Zhang <chan9yan9@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Zexiang Zhang <chan9yan9@gmail.com>
---
 hw/intc/xics.c | 7 +++++++
 1 file changed, 7 insertions(+)

diff --git a/hw/intc/xics.c b/hw/intc/xics.c
index 1d40c4386d..25c7b0c8a5 100644
--- a/hw/intc/xics.c
+++ b/hw/intc/xics.c
@@ -222,6 +222,13 @@ void icp_irq(ICSState *ics, int server, int nr, uint8_t priority)
 
     trace_xics_icp_irq(server, nr, priority);
 
+    if (!icp) {
+        qemu_log_mask(LOG_GUEST_ERROR, "XICS: invalid server %d for IRQ 0x%x\n",
+                      server, nr);
+        ics_reject(ics, nr);
+        return;
+    }
+
     if ((priority >= CPPR(icp))
         || (XISR(icp) && (icp->pending_priority <= priority))) {
         ics_reject(ics, nr);
-- 
2.34.1
disclaimer: LLM-generated draft. Not an authoritative review.
